challenges.prompt: Fill in the selector so this rule targets any element carrying class="badge".
challenges.explanation: A class selector is written by prefixing the class name with a dot. So class="badge" is targeted with .badge. Writing badge with no dot would instead look for a <badge> tag, which does not exist.

challenges.prompt: Complete this rule so it matches only <a> elements that live inside a <nav> element.
challenges.explanation: A single space between two selectors creates a descendant relationship: nav a means "any <a> nested anywhere inside a <nav>". Remove the space (nava) and it becomes one meaningless tag name that matches nothing.

challenges.prompt: This rule is meant to style the single element with id="page-title", but nothing changes. What's wrong?
options: IDs should be selected with a hash (#page-title), not a dot — the dot targets a class
font-size cannot be set in rem units, only in px
An id can only receive one declaration at a time
challenges.explanation: The dot prefix (.) selects elements by class. To target id="page-title" you must use the hash prefix: #page-title. As written, .page-title looks for class="page-title", which the element does not have.

challenges.prompt: Both rules target the same element (it has id="cta" and class="btn"). What color will its text be?
options: White — the ID selector wins because it has higher specificity
Black — the later rule always overrides earlier ones
Grey — the two colors are blended together
challenges.explanation: CSS decides the winner by specificity before source order. An ID selector (#cta) outranks a class selector (.btn), so the text is white even though the class rule comes later in the file. Source order only breaks ties when specificity is equal.
